# Architectural Drawing Description

This is a historical architectural blueprint on aged, beige paper showing detailed construction plans for a large Victorian-era building. The main drawing dominates the lower left portion and depicts a longitudinal (side) elevation view of an elaborate structure featuring multiple stories, a prominent central tower with a pointed spire, gabled roof sections, numerous windows and doors arranged in regular patterns, decorative railings, and various architectural details rendered in brown ink with cross-hatching for shading. The building extends horizontally across the page with varying heights and architectural elements. In the upper right corner is a detailed cross-sectional view labeled "Transverse Section" showing the internal structural framework and spatial relationships within the building. Small handwritten annotations in cursive script appear throughout, including measurements and notes. At the bottom of the main elevation drawing are labeled sections identifying different parts: "Main Front," "House Keeper," "Servants Wing," "Verandah," and "Servants" from left to right. The document shows signs of age with stains and wear marks across the parchment surface. A colored reference scale bar is visible on the right edge of the paper.

# Second Floor Architectural Plan

This is an aged architectural blueprint showing the second floor layout of a building designed by F. McIlhous & Son, Architects, located at 530 Pine Street. The document is drawn at a scale of eight feet to one inch, as indicated at the bottom. A color reference bar with numbered sections appears at the top of the page for measurement purposes.

The plan displays multiple rooms with dimensions marked throughout. The layout shows several "Bed Rooms" (labeled as rooms 1, 3, 4, and 5) with measurements ranging from approximately 11' x 12' to 15' x 16'. Two "Parlor Bedrooms" are prominently featured in the upper portion, each measuring roughly 12' x 20'. The central areas include hallways, with a prominent central hall measuring 16' x 21'. Stairs are shown connecting different sections of the floor, indicated by parallel lines. Bathrooms and additional service areas are distributed throughout the layout with smaller dimensions.

The plan uses brown ink lines to delineate room boundaries and walls, with handwritten notations identifying each space. Diagonal lines in some rooms indicate flooring patterns or design elements. The document shows significant age-related deterioration, including water stains and discoloration across the cream-colored paper.

# Architectural Floor Plan Description

This is a vintage architectural blueprint showing the first floor layout of a multi-story residential building. The document is drawn on aged, cream-colored paper with visible stains and wear marks, and features pen and ink line drawings with handwritten room labels and measurements throughout. A color reference scale and ruler appear at the top of the page for documentation purposes.

The floor plan depicts a symmetrical layout organized around a central hallway and staircase. The ground level includes large formal spaces: an "Old Ladies Dining Room" (30' x 30') and "Children's Dining Room" (30' x 30') positioned on either side of the central axis, with an entrance between them. Upper levels contain numerous smaller rooms including bedrooms, a kitchen, pantries, servants' areas, and various auxiliary spaces. Each room is labeled with its name and dimensions. Multiple staircases and corridors connect the different sections.

The architectural firm responsible for this design is identified as "E. M. Moosgall & Son, Archts." at 330 Pine Street, San Francisco, as noted in the bottom right corner. The notation "Scale: Eight Feet to One Inch" appears at the bottom, indicating the proportional relationship between the drawing and actual construction. The document is titled "First Floor" at the bottom center.
